data.title: GWA Group (ASX:GWA) Dividend Strain Tests Bullish Margin Improvement Narratives
data.description:
GWA Group (ASX:GWA) has put fresh numbers on the table for H1 2026, with revenue of A$208.6 million, basic EPS of A$0.083 and net income of A$21.8 million framing the latest half. The company has seen revenue move from A$207.5 million in H2 2024 to A$209.9 million in H1 2025 and A$208.6 million in H2 2025, while basic EPS has shifted from A$0.058 to A$0.081 and A$0.083 over the same periods. This sets up this result against a backdrop of improving trailing margins and a richer profit pool that investors will be keen to assess.

See our full analysis for GWA Group.

With the headline figures set, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing narratives around GWA’s earnings strength, margin quality and outlook, and where those stories might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about GWA Group

Advertisement

Margins Hold Around 10.4% Net Level

  • Over the last 12 months, GWA reported net income of A$46.6 million on revenue of A$422.7 million, which works out to a 10.4% net profit margin compared with 9.3% in the prior year period.
  • Analysts' consensus view refers to higher margins being supported by water efficient products and digital initiatives. However, the margin shift from 9.3% to 10.4% also sits against risks such as pricing pressure and cost challenges that could limit how far those margins can move.

Trailing EPS And P/E Compared With Peers

  • Trailing twelve month basic EPS sits at A$0.1765, and with the current share price at A$2.62, that implies a trailing P/E of 15.8x, which is below the cited 16.6x peer average and 20.1x global building industry level.
  • Consensus narrative highlights long term demand from urbanisation and housing shortages. At the same time, the current 15.8x P/E and analysts' price target of A$2.98 leave a gap that investors have to weigh against the fact that earnings grew 12.3% over the past year from a lower base.

Dividend Yield Versus Earnings Coverage

  • The dividend yield of 5.92% sits alongside trailing net income of A$46.6 million and a 10.4% margin, and the payout is flagged as not well covered by earnings over the last 12 months.
  • What stands out against the analysts' consensus narrative is that while they point to strong cash generation and low leverage as support for shareholder returns, the weak earnings coverage of a near 6% yield shows a tension between maintaining that payout and the revenue growth assumptions of 5.9% a year and margin expectations of 12% in three years.

data.title: Is UiPath (PATH) Starting To Look Attractive After Recent Share Price Weakness
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether UiPath is starting to look interesting at current levels, this article will walk through what the recent share price and fundamentals might be telling you about its value.
  • UiPath shares last closed at US$11.32, with returns of 12.9% decline over 7 days, 21.1% decline over 30 days, 28.7% decline year to date, 24.2% decline over 1 year and 27.2% decline over 3 years, which may have shifted how the market is thinking about its risk and potential.
  • Recent headlines around UiPath have focused on its position in the automation software space and how investors are reacting to changing expectations for growth and profitability, especially as sentiment across software names has been reassessed. This backdrop helps explain why some investors are rechecking whether the current price fairly reflects the company’s long term prospects.
  • On our framework of six valuation checks, UiPath scores 4 out of 6, suggesting some parts of the market may see it as undervalued on several measures. Next, we will look at how different valuation methods line up on that score, before finishing with an approach that many investors find even more useful for understanding value.

Find out why UiPath's -24.2% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: UiPath Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects the cash a business is expected to generate in the future and then discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the entire company might be worth right now.

For UiPath, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity DCF. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $319.9 million. Analysts provide detailed estimates for the early years, and for later years Simply Wall St extrapolates those cash flows. On this basis, projected free cash flow in 2035 is $689.6 million, with each year between 2026 and 2035 contributing a discounted amount to the valuation.

When you add up all those discounted cash flows, the model arrives at an intrinsic value estimate of about US$18.31 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$11.32, this implies an intrinsic discount of 38.2%, which, on this DCF view, suggests that UiPath stock may be trading below this estimate of intrinsic value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests UiPath is undervalued by 38.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 54 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for UiPath.

Approach 2: UiPath Price vs Earnings

For a business that is generating earnings, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge what the market is currently willing to pay for each dollar of profit. It quickly links the share price to the company’s earnings, which is often described as a key driver of long term returns.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher uncertainty typically support a lower one.

UiPath is trading on a P/E of 26.36x. That sits close to the broader Software industry average of 26.36x and below the peer group average of 30.23x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for UiPath is 12.73x, which is its proprietary estimate of a suitable P/E given factors such as earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

Because the Fair Ratio incorporates those company specific drivers, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with industry or peer averages. With UiPath’s current P/E of 26.36x above the Fair Ratio of 12.73x, the shares appear expensive on this metric.

data.title: Should You Buy Apollo Global Management, Inc. (NYSE:APO) For Its Upcoming Dividend?
data.description:
Apollo Global Management, Inc. (NYSE:APO) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 2 days. The ex-dividend date is one business day before the record date, which is the cut-off date for shareholders to be present on the company's books to be eligible for a dividend payment. The ex-dividend date is important as the process of settlement involves a full business day. So if you miss that date, you would not show up on the company's books on the record date. Accordingly, Apollo Global Management investors that purchase the stock on or after the 19th of February will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 27th of February.

The company's next dividend payment will be US$0.51 per share, on the back of last year when the company paid a total of US$2.04 to shareholders. Last year's total dividend payments show that Apollo Global Management has a trailing yield of 1.6% on the current share price of US$125.07. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! So we need to check whether the dividend payments are covered, and if earnings are growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Fortunately Apollo Global Management's payout ratio is modest, at just 35% of profit.

Generally speaking, the lower a company's payout ratios, the more resilient its dividend usually is.

See our latest analysis for Apollo Global Management

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. That's why it's comforting to see Apollo Global Management's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 68% per annum for the past five years.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. Apollo Global Management's dividend payments per share have declined at 3.4% per year on average over the past 10 years, which is uninspiring. It's unusual to see earnings per share increasing at the same time as dividends per share have been in decline. We'd hope it's because the company is reinvesting heavily in its business, but it could also suggest business is lumpy.

The Bottom Line

Is Apollo Global Management worth buying for its dividend? When companies are growing rapidly and retaining a majority of the profits within the business, it's usually a sign that reinvesting earnings creates more value than paying dividends to shareholders. Perhaps even more importantly - this can sometimes signal management is focused on the long term future of the business. In summary, Apollo Global Management appears to have some promise as a dividend stock, and we'd suggest taking a closer look at it.

In light of that, while Apollo Global Management has an appealing dividend, it's worth knowing the risks involved with this stock. For example, we've found 3 warning signs for Apollo Global Management that we recommend you consider before investing in the business.

Price to earnings of 27.4x: Is it justified?

On a P/E of 27.4x, Guoquan Food (Shanghai) is priced above the wider Asian consumer retailing industry average of 17.5x, even after the recent share price pull back to HK$3.87.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price with earnings per share, so a higher multiple usually reflects the market paying more today for each unit of profit. For a home meal products and retail business like Guoquan, that often ties back to how confident investors are about future earnings, store economics and the sustainability of margin trends.

Here, the picture is mixed. On one hand, earnings have grown by 50.5% over the past year and by 48% per year over the past 5 years, with net profit margins currently at 4.7% compared with 3.6% last year. Analysts also expect earnings to grow about 26.5% per year over the next 3 years, which is faster than the wider Hong Kong market, and revenue growth of 16.2% per year is forecast to outpace the local market rate of 8.5%. On the other hand, the current 27.4x P/E is higher than the company’s own estimated fair P/E of 25.8x, and return on equity is 11%, which is flagged as low compared with a 20% benchmark.

Versus peers, Guoquan looks expensive against the broader Asian consumer retailing industry multiple of 17.5x. However, it screens as better value when compared to its direct peer group average multiple of 37.9x. That gap between the current 27.4x and the fair P/E estimate of 25.8x is also a level the market could move towards if sentiment or growth expectations change, so the current premium is not extreme but it is there.
